title: Complete Unauthenticated User Data Exposure via IDOR
description: The /user/profile/{userID} functionality lacks authorization controls for profile access, allowing unauthenticated enumeration of user accounts and disclosure of sensitive personal and financial information. The reports identify GET /user/profile/{userID} as directly enumerable, and also list related profile endpoints such as PUT /user/profile/{userID} and PUT /user/profile/{userID}/password as affected by missing authorization. Exposed data included usernames, account details, and plaintext passwords.
steps: Send unauthenticated GET requests to user profile endpoints, for example: GET http://finance:8000/user/profile/1 and GET http://finance:8000/user/profile/2. Continue enumerating user IDs and observe that profile data is returned without authentication.

title: Unauthorized Loan Disbursement via Transaction Manipulation
description: The loan processing endpoint POST /bank/loan/request lacks proper authorization checks, allowing unauthorized users to manipulate financial transactions and obtain loans. The assessment documented a successful unauthorized loan approval resulting in $1,200 in financial impact.
steps: Authenticate as any valid user, then submit a POST request to http://finance:8000/bank/loan/request with loan data such as amount, purpose, and duration. Observe that the loan request is approved without adequate authorization verification.

title: Authentication Credentials Transmitted and Stored in Plaintext
description: User credentials are exposed in API responses in plaintext, and the report states credentials are transmitted and stored without encryption. Passwords were directly visible in user profile responses, enabling immediate account compromise.
steps: Access user profile data, such as through the IDOR on /user/profile/{userID}, and inspect the JSON response. Observe plaintext password values returned directly in the response.

title: Multiple SQL Injection Points Across Critical Endpoints
description: Several application endpoints were reported as vulnerable to SQL injection. The source reports differ on the exact affected endpoints: report.md mentions registration, login, and financial endpoints, with examples including POST /auth/register and GET /bank/transactions/history?account={injection}; final_report identifies POST /auth/login, POST /bank/loan/request, and POST /bank/get-credit-card. Testing included error-based, UNION-based, and time-based payloads, with reported impact including database structure exposure and potential data extraction.
steps: Submit SQL injection payloads to reported parameters, such as POST /auth/register, POST /auth/login, GET /bank/transactions/history?account=<payload>, POST /bank/loan/request, or POST /bank/get-credit-card, depending on the tested endpoint. Use payloads like test', UNION SELECT, or time-based payloads and observe SQL errors or other injection indicators.

title: Absence of Authorization Controls Across User Management Endpoints
description: Critical user management functions lack authorization verification, allowing unauthorized modification of user data and account takeover actions. The reports specifically identify endpoints such as PUT /user/profile/{userID}, PUT /user/profile/{userID}/password, and POST /user/delete-account as lacking proper authorization checks for sensitive state-changing operations.
steps: Send unauthorized requests to state-changing user management endpoints, such as PUT http://finance:8000/user/profile/1 with modified profile data or PUT http://finance:8000/user/profile/1/password with a new password. Observe that the application accepts the changes without proper authorization verification.

title: Excessive API Endpoint Exposure Without Proper Access Controls
description: Numerous sensitive API endpoints are exposed without adequate authentication or authorization requirements, expanding the attack surface and creating potential unauthorized access paths. Reported examples include GET /bank/transactions/history?account={account}, GET /bank/loan/user?userID={userID}, POST /auth/register, POST /user/reset-password, POST /user/delete-account, POST /bank/account, and POST /bank/get-credit-card.
steps: Enumerate and directly access sensitive API endpoints and verify that functionality or information is exposed without sufficient access controls. Examples include /bank/transactions/history, /bank/loan/user, /user/reset-password, /user/delete-account, /bank/account, and /bank/get-credit-card.

title: Multiple Security Control Deficiencies
description: The application is missing several baseline security controls, creating defense-in-depth weaknesses and increasing exploitability. The reports group these issues into a single low-severity finding, including no rate limiting or enumeration protection, no account lockout mechanisms, permissive CORS configuration, lack of input validation, and insufficient error handling.
steps: Review application behavior and HTTP responses to confirm missing controls. Repeated requests are not rate limited and do not trigger account lockout, responses indicate permissive CORS settings, malformed or malicious input is insufficiently validated, and error handling reveals weaknesses during testing.
